title: Dog Cat Scratch on Eyeball: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog's eye was scratched by a cat, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn about corneal ulcers and why immediate vet care is essential.
content_html:
What is a Corneal Scratch?

A cat scratch to a dog's eyeball typically results in a corneal ulcer or a corneal laceration. The cornea is the clear, sensitive window at the front of the eye. Because cat claws are sharp and carry significant bacteria, these injuries are not just physical trauma but also highly prone to aggressive infections that can threaten your dog's sight.

Is This an Emergency?

Yes. The urgency level is High. Any direct trauma to the globe of the eye is considered a medical emergency. Eye conditions can deteriorate with incredible speed; a minor scratch can turn into a melting ulcer or a ruptured eyeball within hours. Prompt veterinary intervention is required to manage pain and prevent permanent blindness or the loss of the eye itself.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear, well-lit photo of the injured eye to your veterinarian or a triage professional can be life-saving. A photo allows the medical team to see the location of the scratch, the level of cloudiness, and whether there is any visible 'flare' or depth to the wound. This helps them prioritize your pet's arrival and prepare the necessary diagnostic tools, such as fluorescein stain, before you even walk through the door.

faqs.question: Can I wait until morning to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. Eye injuries are extremely painful and can progress to a point of no return overnight. Seeking emergency care immediately is the safest course of action.

faqs.question: Should I use human eye drops to help with the redness?
faqs.answer: Absolutely not. Many human eye drops contain steroids or other ingredients that can cause a scratched cornea to 'melt' or worsen the infection, leading to a rupture.

faqs.question: How will the vet treat the scratch?
faqs.answer: The vet will likely use a special dye to see the extent of the damage, prescribe pet-safe antibiotics, and provide pain relief. In severe cases, surgery may be required.
